role_summary: Civil rights physician; founder of the Tufts-Delta Health Center at Mound Bayou, Mississippi (1965) and the federal community-health-center model; co-founder of Physicians for Human Rights; founding chair of CUNY Medical School's Community Health and Social Medicine department.
ai_reading: Geiger's top semantic neighbor is Alfred Moldovan (entry 133) at a cosine count of 443, a remarkably high pairing given Moldovan is one of the catalog's ingestion-only-tier entries and culturally less prominent. The pairing suggests the embedding finds the medical-civil-rights vocabulary across both interviews as the dominant vector signature, more than either's institutional affiliation or political framing. His next neighbors are William G. Anderson (entry 126), Charles McLaurin (Mississippi SNCC, entry 17), Robert L. Carter (NAACP LDF, entry 108), and Maria Varela (SNCC photographer, entry 81). The cluster crosses medical-and-organizational categories, reading Geiger as a movement physician whose 1965 line that "the specific therapy for malnutrition is, in fact, food" sits in the same conceptual space as the Mississippi voter-registration cohort's work on basic-needs organizing. His axis positions all fall within +/-0.21 of zero, placing him among the catalog's most centrist voices alongside McDew, Terry, Richardson, and Noonan.
biographical_paragraph: Geiger took an MD at Case Western Reserve University School of Medicine in 1958 and a master's in epidemiology at the Harvard School of Public Health [src: 2]. In 1965 he founded the Tufts-Delta Health Center at Mound Bayou, Mississippi, one of the first federally funded community health centers; the model he developed for low-income community medical care became the basis for what is now a national network of community health centers [src: 2]. He later helped found Physicians for Human Rights and was the founding chair of CUNY Medical School's Community Health and Social Medicine department [src: 2]. His own often-quoted 1965 line, "the specific therapy for malnutrition is, in fact, food," framed his prescription-pad practice at Mound Bayou of writing food orders that the federal health center paid for out of its pharmacy budget [src: 2]. He was born November 11, 1925 in Brooklyn, New York, and died December 28, 2020 in Brooklyn at ninety-five; the New York Times obituary by Denise Grady is the standard published record of his life [src: 2]. His Library of Congress oral history is entry 54 of the Civil Rights History Project (American Folklife Center accession AFC 2010/039); the interview was conducted by historian John Dittmer in 2013 [src: 1].

sources.title: H. Jack Geiger oral history (interview by John Dittmer, 2013)
sources.publisher: Civil Rights History Project, Library of Congress (AFC 2010/039)

sources.title: Denise Grady, Dr. H. Jack Geiger, Doctor Who Fought Social Ills, Dies at 95
